coredata.dc:title: Tuning photoluminescence of boron nitride quantum dots via surface functionalization by femtosecond laser ablation
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Vanthan
Yan, Lihe
Zhao, Na
Van Canh, Nguyen
Hang, Nguyen Thi Nhat
Le, Phuoc Huu
coredata.dc:description: Tuning the Photoluminescence (PL) properties of boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) is crucial for determining PL mechanism and subsequently obtaining BNQDs with intense and desired PL emission. Here, we propose a facile one-step method to synthesize and functionalize BNQDs with tunable PL from ultraviolet to green region using femtosecond laser ablation in organic solution. Detailed characterization suggests that carbene zigzag edges are formed on the surface of BNQDs and result in their effective emission. The emission colors for the BNQDs redshift by increasing content of carboxyl group on the carbene zigzag edges. This work presents a promising strategy for synthesis and functionalization of BNQDs and provides a deeper understanding of the origin of their PL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructured materials
Optical materials
Surfaces and interfaces
Carbene zigzag edges
Laser processing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Origin of the tunable photoluminescence of BNQDs
Potential application in bioimaging of BNQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of BNQDs. (a, b, c) PL spectra of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs, respectively. (d) Two-photon PL spectrum and photograph (inset) of g-BNQDs under 800 nm femtosecond laser excitation. (e) Quadratic relationship of the fluorescence intensity of the g-BNQDs aqueous solution with the different excitation laser powers at 800 nm. (f) UV-visible absorption spectra of BNQDs.

Morphological properties of BNQDs. (a, b, c) TEM images of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs, respectively. Insets are their corresponding size distributions. (d, e, f) Representative HRTEM images of the BNQDs with lattice distance and corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns of the edges.

Chemical structures of BNQDs. (a) FTIR spectra of BNQDs. (b) XPS full survey spectra of BNQDs. (c) High-resolution B 1 s XPS spectrum of g-BNQDs. (d, e, f) High-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs, respectively. (g, h, i) High-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs, respectively.

One-photon and two-photon confocal fluorescence images of isolated g-BNQDs. (a) One-photon fluorescence image of g-BNQDs excited at 408 nm with green emission (499 ―529 nm). (b) Two-photon fluorescence image of g-BNQDs under 800 nm excitation with detected wavelength of 350–560 nm.

head.ce:title: Tuning photoluminescence of boron nitride quantum dots via surface functionalization by femtosecond laser ablation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Physics and Biophysics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy, 179 Nguyen Van Cu Street, Can Tho, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tuning the Photoluminescence (PL) properties of boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) is crucial for determining PL mechanism and subsequently obtaining BNQDs with intense and desired PL emission. Here, we propose a facile one-step method to synthesize and functionalize BNQDs with tunable PL from ultraviolet to green region using femtosecond laser ablation in organic solution. Detailed characterization suggests that carbene zigzag edges are formed on the surface of BNQDs and result in their effective emission. The emission colors for the BNQDs redshift by increasing content of carboxyl group on the carbene zigzag edges. This work presents a promising strategy for synthesis and functionalization of BNQDs and provides a deeper understanding of the origin of their PL.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) derived from the two-dimensional (2D) materials, particularly heavy-metal-free nanocrystals, have attracted increasing interest recently due to their unique and fascinating physicochemical properties [1]. As compared with their native nanosheet forms, these QDs offer even higher special surface area and more edge atoms, better dispersibility, higher tunability in physiochemical properties, and more easy to be functionalized which are crucial for many applications [1]. Graphene quantum dots, a representative example, are promising substitutes for the conventional organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots in wide range applications including imaging, sensing, optoelectronic, catalysis, and energy [2, 3]. Compared with the already investigated graphene quantum dots, boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) are relatively a new member of 2D QDs, which are much less explored. Combining with intrinsic properties of boron nitride (hBN), high thermal conductivity, superior chemical stability, excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity, and good dispersibility [4–9], BNQDs have been proven to be promising candidates in biological and optoelectronic applications [5,7,10–15].

Synthesis and tuning the photoluminescence (PL) properties of BNQDs are crucial for determining PL mechanism and subsequently preparing BNQDs with intense and desired PL emission. It is expected that PL properties can be tailored, and many brand-new features and applications can be created directly via its smart functionalization [16,17]. Actually, functionalization of carbon-based materials can refer to many organic chemical reactions while the highly chemical stability/inertness of h-BN hinders its modifications [18]. To date, the BNQDs can be functionalized by few methods such as hydrothermal or microwave irradiation treatment of hBN nanosheets in organic solution [7,11,19]. Some researcher reported the successful synthesis and functionalization of BNQDs via one-step hydrothermal of organic precursors [9,10]. Although BNQDs with tunable PL have been successfully obtained via surface functionalization, most of the synthesis process is typically multi-step and long-time [7,11].

Laser Ablation in Solution (LAS) is a simple, fast, and “green” approach for synthesis of various nanomaterials including graphene QDs [20], carbon nanodots [21,22], noble metal nanoparticle [23], and transition metal dicalchogenides QDs [24]. In LAS, the nanomaterials are obtained by focusing laser pulses on a target immersed in a liquid solution. Comparison with bulk target, laser ablation of dispersed powder in solution has the unique advantages of small size distribution and more uniform surface structure of prepared nanomaterials. During laser ablation, active species can react with the molecules in the liquid, and nanomaterials with complex functionalization can be obtained in one-step by adding selected ligands or ablation in different liquids [25,26].

Here, we report the one-step synthesis and functionalization of BNQDs with tunable PL from ultraviolet to green region by femtosecond laser ablation method for the first time. Three types of BNQDs emitting ultraviolet, blue, and green PL were synthesized by laser ablation of hBN powder in ethanol, diethylamine (DEA), and ethylenediamine (EDA), respectively. Carbene zigzag edges were formed on the surface of BNQDs and result in their effective emission. Because of their excellent PL properties, these BNQDs could be used to as fluorescence probe for bioimaging.

BNQDs were synthesized via femtosecond laser ablation of hBN powders in ethanol, diethylamine (DEA), and ethylenediamine (EDA), respectively. The detailed procedure is similar with that of our previous study on carbon nanodots [27,28]. Typically, 20 mg of hBN powder, with size ~2 µm, was dispersed into 50 ml of organic solution via ultrasonication (Scientz-750F) for 20 min. Next, ~10 ml of the suspension was put into a glass beaker for laser irradiation. The laser beam (Ti: sapphire femtosecond laser system, central wavelength: 800 nm, pulse duration: 150 fs, and repetition rate: 1 kHz) was focused into suspension at a laser fluence of 200 J/cm2 by 25 mm lens for 30 min. During the laser irradiation, a magnetic stirrer was used to prevent gravitational settling of the suspended powders [27]. After laser irradiation, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and the BNQDs were obtained from the supernatant.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the BNQDs were obtained via a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (model FEI TITAN G2). The Raman spectra were acquired from a Raman System (HR800) with a 532 nm laser excitation. A UV–2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) was employed to measure the absorption spectra of the samples. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a VERTEX 70 (Bruker) using KBr pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out on AXIS Ultra-DLD (Kratos) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a detection limit around 0.1 at.%. The XPS spectra were analyzed using CasaXPS software (V2.3.16, Casa Software Ltd.). The high-resolution spectra were fitted using Gaussian-Lorentzian (GL(30)) line-shape after Shirley background subtraction. The parameters LOD_ESTIMATE and LOD_AREA were included in the region configuration file of CasaXPS software to get the value of the limit of detection (LOD). When assessing if the peak is detectable, the ratio of LOD_AREA and LOD_ESTIMATE should be greater than 3 [29]. The PL characterizations including emission spectra, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, and time-resolved PL spectra were recorded using FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh) [30]. For the time-resolved PL measurements, picosecond pulsed LEDs (central wavelengths: 273, 343, and 405 nm, pulse duration: < 850 ps, repetition rate: 10 MHz) were used as excitation sources. Two-photon PL spectra were recorded using USB2000+ spectrometer (Ocean Optics) with 800 nm femtosecond laser excitation (Ti: sapphire femtosecond laser system).

Fluorescence images of BNQDs were obtained using confocal laser scanning microscope. BNQDs were immobilized on cleaned glass coverslip by dropping a small droplet of BNQDs solution on coverslip. One-photon fluorescence images were obtained via an inverted confocal microscope (Nikon C2+). Laser beam (408 nm) was focused on the sample via 60× oil objective (1.49 NA) at laser intensity of ~ 5 mW. The fluorescence emission was harvested at 499 - 529 nm for green channel [30]. Two-photon fluorescence image was obtained via multiphoton laser scanning microscope (OLYMPUS FV1000MPE) with 60× water objective (1.2 NA). The excitation wavelength was 800 nm and the emission range for green channel was 350–560 nm.

The BNQDs prepared in ethanol, DEA, and EDA exhibited intense PL in near ultraviolet, blue, and green region, respectively (Supporting information, Fig. S1). The BNQDs were named as uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs for those obtained using ethanol, DEA, and EDA as solution, respectively. The fluorescence peaks of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs were centered at about 325, 405, 480 nm in an ethanol solution under 280, 360, and 460 nm excitations, respectively (Fig. 1 (a) – (c)). uv-BNQDs and b-BNQDs showed nearly excitation-independent emission, while g-BNQDs showed slightly excitation-dependent emission peak at around 480 nm. The excitation-independent property is popular in other fluorescence nanomaterials such as carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots [31,32], but rare in BNQDs [12], In addition, control experiments of femtosecond laser ablation of pure organic solutions were carried out to exclude the possibility of generating nanodiamonds from ultrafast laser irradiation of liquid precursor [33]. The PL emissions of obtained solutions were not detectable (Fig. S2), indicating that the intense PL were originated from BNQDs. The PL quantum yields were determined to be 3.8%, 22.8%, and 18.4% for uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs in ethanol at their optimal excitation wavelengths, respectively (using quinine sulfate as the standard sample). Besides one-photon PL, the as-prepared BNQDs exhibited bright two-photon fluorescence emission. The g-BNQDs solutions emit bright two-photon PL under an 800 nm femtosecond pulsed laser excitation (Fig. 1(d)). The obvious quadratic relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the excited laser power suggests the two-photon excitation was truly responsible for the present emission (Fig. 1(e)).

The UV−visible absorption spectra of the BNQDs displayed analogous absorption in the UV region of 200−250 nm but different behaviors in the lower-energy region (as shown in Fig. 1(f)). Besides an absorption peak around 275 nm, b-BNQDs showed another absorption peak around 325 nm, while g-BNQDs showed a peak around 350 nm with a tail up to 450 nm. These absorption peaks in lower-energy region can be assigned to π electron transition of oxygen-containing functional groups in the edges of BNQDs due to their high interaction with the solvent [17]. The different UV–visible absorptions for the three BNQDs might be resulted from the different surface chemical functionalities in which QDs with abundant surface functional groups will possess broad and strong absorption band in low-energy region. Therefore, it appears that the optical properties of the prepared BNQDs in different solvents are distinct due to the interaction of solvent molecules with BN during the synthesis process. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of the BNQDs detected at strongest fluorescence show two evident peaks located at around 309, 360 nm and 438, 465 nm for b-BNQDs and g-BNQDs, respectively (Fig. S3). The maximum peaks in PLE spectra are in the region of absorption bands in UV-visible absorption spectra indicating that these absorption bands is corresponding to the strong PL peak.

The TEM images reveal that the BNQDs were well dispersed with small size distribution in the range of 0.5 – 2.5 nm (Fig. 2 (a) – (c)). The BNQDs distribute homogeneously on the copper grid and no aggregation can be observed. The average diameters of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs were 1.4, 1.8, and 1.6 nm, respectively (Insets of Fig. 2(a) – (c)). The TEM data suggest that the differences in PL emission of BNQDs are not due to the differences in their particle size, i.e. quantum confinement effect cannot be ascribed to the red shift in PL emission of the BNQDs. Raman spectrum of as-prepared BNQDs showed a much weaker E2g phonon mode at around 1366 cm−1 compared with that of raw hBN powder (Fig. S4). The significant decrease of this mode is consistent with previous reported results and also suggests the few-layer structure of as-prepared BNQDs [6, 15]. The HRTEM images reveal that the BNQDs have good crystallinity with lattice of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice space of the hBN (Fig. 2(d)). The edge structures, i.e. zigzag edges are evident based on their HRTEM images and fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns of the edges as shown in Fig. 2(e), (f). The edge structures presented here are similar with that of graphene-based nanomaterials such as graphene, graphene oxide, and graphene quantum dots [34–36].

In order to shed light on the origin of their PL property, the chemical compositions and structures of these BNQDs were further investigated using FTIR and XPS analysis. The FTIR spectra of the BNQDs (Fig. 3 (a)) demonstrate that different category and quantity of surface functional groups were created on the surfaces of these BNQDs. All BNQDs samples showed absorption bands of O―H/N―H (3360 cm−1), C―H (2920 and 2850 cm−1), C―N (1700 - 1750 cm−1), and B―N (1375 cm−1) bonds. Clearly, the absorption of C―H peak is dominant in all samples. Furthermore, the quantity of O―H/N―H and C―N bonds in g-BNQDs was larger than that in uv-BNQDs and b-BNQDs, indicating more abundant surface functional groups were created in g-BNQDs. In addition, the O―H vibration bands were sharp and discrete for uv-BNQDs and b-BNQDs but broad and integrated for g-BNQDs, suggesting that multiple structures of hydroxyl groups were created on the surfaces of g-BNQDs [31].

The surface structures of the BNQDs were further confirmed by XPS characterization. Full XPS survey spectra of three BNQDs samples showed three strong peaks corresponding to carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements (Fig. 3(b)). The peak of boron element also can be detected but its content was much smaller than that of C and O elements (< 1,2%, Table S1), and the high-resolution spectra were hard to record. Furthermore, large content of carbon (> 85%) indicate that the surface of BNQDs were coated with abundant surface functional groups. There are two peaks of B―O (191.1 eV) and B―N (187,1 eV) in high-resolution spectrum of the B 1 s (Fig. 3(c)). High-resolution C 1 s spectra of all BNQDs samples revealed the presence of C―C/C = C (284.6 eV), C―N/C―O (286.0 eV), and C = O (288.3 eV) (Fig. 3(d) – (f)) [30]. Noticeably, C = O bonds in C 1 s spectra for uv-BNQDs and b-BNQDs showed low content of about 2% while that in g-BNQDs was increasing to 10.74% (Table S2). The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CN/CO fitting peak for the BNQDs were in the range of 2.0–2.4 eV. The broadening of peaks can be attributed to the presence of complex functional groups on the surface of BNQDs. The absence of C―B bonding energy in C 1 s spectra suggests functional groups were attached on the surface of BNQDs via C―N bonds. The N 1 s spectra of all samples (Fig. 1(g) – (i)) showed three peaks at 398.8, 399.8, and 401.5 eV, which are attributed to N―B, N―O/N―C, and N―H bonds, respectively. Similarly, the content of N―O/N―C gradually increased from uv-BNQDs to g-BNQDs. The XPS data was consistent with the FTIR results; clearly demonstrate that abundant carbon- and oxygen-containing functional groups were attached on the surface of BNQDs and the contents of carboxyl groups increased concurrently with the red shift of PL.

Based on the aforementioned results, we give a brief interpretation on the possible mechanism for BNQDs generation in femtosecond laser ablation. When the femtosecond pulses inject into the solid targets, Coulomb explosion takes place and a plasma plume with high temperature and high pressure are formed due to multiphoton absorption ionization. Subsequently, the plasma plume expands and cools down generating a cavitation bubble in the solution. With the expansion of the bubble, the BN nanoclusters with high surface energy in cavitation bubble tend to aggregate into larger nanoparticles and BNQDs with small size are formed [21,25,26]. Since the laser-induced high-density plasma is in the state with high temperature and high pressure [37], liquid molecules are excited and dissociated at the interface between the plasma and the liquid. Subsequently, dramatically chemical reaction between ablated species and reactive species from plasma-induced decomposition of organic solution occurs inside the plasma plume, at the interface between the plasma and the liquid, or inside the liquid [37]. Due to the reactive species from plasma-induced decomposition of organic solution are distinct from different organic sources, BNQDs with different surface chemical structure can be obtained by using different organic solution. Furthermore, because of our laser ablation process was carried out under air and oxygen in the air can be introduced into solution resulting in the presence of oxygen element in the BNQDs. Therefore, the surface of BNQDs was mainly composition of abundant surface functional groups with small amount of boron element. However, the contribution of boron element was more evident after exfoliate the BNQDs to monolayer shape in which more boron atoms are appearing in the surface [38].

In addition to surface chemistry, the size of BNQDs can be effectively controlled by changing the organic solutions. The properties of the solution such as viscosity, polarity, and chemical structure play an important role in determining the properties of the cavitation bubble, such as volume, expansion velocity, and lifetime. Subsequently, the nucleation and growth mechanism of the QDs are affected, and the final size of QDs can be tuned [25]. Due to the lower viscosity of DEA (0.319 mPa s, 25 ºC), the confinement of plasma plume and cavitation bubble in DEA will be weaker than that in the two others, which leads to a longer lifetime of cavitation bubble and facilitate formation of larger BNQDs. Furthermore, refraction at the air-liquid boundary, light absorption of the liquid, self-focusing, and optical breakdown in the liquid reduce overall laser energy input to the dispersed particles and reduce the ablation efficiency [25]. Due to lower light absorption and self-focusing of ethanol, the ablation efficiency of hBN in ethanol is higher than that in the two others. As a result, already-size-reduced BNQDs will be further ablated to generate BNQDs with small size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin of the tunable photoluminescence of BNQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hexagonal boron nitride is a wide bandgap semiconductor with deep ultraviolet emission around 215 nm [39,40]. In spite of the rising interest in hBN and the large number of studies devoted to this material, the basic questions of the bandgap nature and value are still controversial [39,41]. hBN monolayer was recently reported to possess mid-gap states in the bandgap due to the existence of defects and display photoluminescence from ultraviolet to near-infrared range [42]. Further reduction of the lateral size of hBN structures to form BNQDs should, in principle, allow the edge and defect dependent properties to become much more evident. Recent studies demonstrated that there are three types of effective emission centers in BNQDs including carbon-replaced N vacancy point defects (3-B and 1-B centers), carbene structure at zigzag edges, and BOx ― (x = 1 and 2) species [5,6,11,19]. Normally, it is difficult to separate the specific fluorescence contributions from these emission centers from the broad emission peak observed in the BNQDs. According to the above characterization, we propose that the emission from carbene structure at zigzag edges may be dominant in the BNQDs prepared by laser ablation presented here. Firstly, we can exclude the possible fluorescence contribution of carbon-replaced N vacancy point defects and BOx ― species in BNQDs due to the absent of C―B bonding energy in C 1 s spectra and small content of B element as well as B―O bonding in high-resolution B 1 s spectra. Secondly, the as-prepared BNQDs were coated with abundant surface functional groups, which can form carbene structure at zigzag edges as evident in HRTEM images [18,43]. Because the triple carbene is most common at zigzag edges [44], the two electronic transitions observed in the PLE spectra of b-BNQDs and g-BNQDs (Fig. S3) can be considered as a transition from the σ and π orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [36,45]. The carbene ground-state multiplicity is related to energy differences (δE) between the σ and π orbital. For a triplet ground state, δE should be below 1.5 eV [46]. In this work, the δE for b-BNQDs and g-BNQDs are 0.57 and 0.16 eV, respectively. The data show that the δE within the required value for triple carbene which suggests that the assignment of the two transitions is reasonable. The results suggest that the PL in BNQDs is the irradiation decay of activated electrons from the LUMO to the HOMO. The results are further supported by similar PL profile of b-BNQDs with that of poly-aromatic molecules such as naphthalene and anthracene [47], which emission peaks arising from HOMO-LUMO levels.

Lastly, several reports ascribed the PL red shift of BNQDs to the decrease of QDs size [6,7]. In the present work, all BNQDs samples exhibited similar size distribution with slightly different in average size. Furthermore, the PL redshift as an increasing contents of carboxyl functional groups on the surface structure. Previously reports suggested that the LUMO – HOMO gaps of carbene zigzag edges can be effectively adjusted by bonding to foreign functional groups such as ―C = O, ―COOH [35,48,49]. In general, carboxyl group can effectively redshift the emission center in graphene-based nanomaterials [31,50,51]. Therefore, we believe that the PL redshift in BNQDs presented here can be attributed to the increasing content of carboxyl group on the carbene zigzag edges. The effects of surface functional groups on PL behavior of BNQDs were further investigated using time-resolved PL spectra. The BNQDs exhibited different fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S5). The fluorescence lifetimes for both uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs were fitted satisfactorily with double-exponential functions, while that for g-BNQDs was fitted with a triple-exponential function (Table S3). This suggests that the decay process is dominated by contributions from different emissive centers. The average lifetimes of uv-BNQDs, b-BNQDs, and g-BNQDs at their optimal excitation wavelengths were 8.3, 1.1, and 2.4 ns, respectively (Table S3). The obvious difference in fluorescence lifetime of the BNQDs can be attributed to the effects of different surface functional groups, in which the competition among various emission centers (edge states) and traps dominates the optical properties of BNQDs [49,52]. Better understanding of the carrier dynamics in BNQDs with advanced spectroscopic techniques will be a subject of future studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential application in bioimaging of BNQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the capability of the BNQDs for bioimaging, we carried out one- and two-photon fluorescence imaging of individual BNQDs using one-photon and multiphoton laser scanning microscopes. The BNQDs were dispersed on glass coverslip used as a substrate in low concentration to allow confocal microscopy imaging of individual QDs. The deposition conditions for the preparation of the specimens were essentially the same as those for TEM imaging. The representative fluorescence image of the g-BNQDs particles excited at 408 nm with green emission (499–529 nm) demonstrates high resolution and a high signal-to-noise ratio as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Besides, the monodispersed g-BNQDs particles sample can also emit a bright two-photon fluorescence signal with detected wavelength of 350–560 nm under 800 nm excitation (Fig. 4(b)). Therefore, the bright one- and two-photon fluorescence in individual BNQDs make these QDs particularly valuable for optical bioimaging in vitro and in vivo, especially with regard to the emerging needs for molecular probes in high-resolution cellular imaging and deep-tissue imaging.

We report the preparation of three BNQDs with tunable photoluminescence from ultraviolet to green region by femtosecond laser ablation of hBN powder in different solutions. Carbene zigzag edges as well as abundant functional groups were generated on the surface of BNQDs by one-step. The bright PL in BNQDs is proposed to originate from triple carbene zigzag edges. Detailed characterization proved that the redshift in the PL emission for the BNQDs results from increasing content of carboxyl group on the carbene zigzag edges. This work presents a promising strategy for the preparation of BNQDs with desired PL properties through surface-functionalization and provides a deeper understanding of the origin of PL in BNQDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported the by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61690221, and 11674260), the Key research and development program of Shaanxi province (Grant No. 2017ZDXM-GY-120).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.130922.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots derived from two-dimensional materials and their applications for catalysis and energy
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, G. Sun, N. Li, P. Chen, Quantum dots derived from two-dimensional materials and their applications for catalysis and energy, Chem. Soc. Rev.45 (2016) 2239–2262.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Chem. Commun.

ACS Nano

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication and luminescence of monolayered boron nitride quantum dots
